commentaries.author: Andreas of Caesarea
commentaries.content: wept. perhaps since the most spotless order of the angelic substances fell into ignorance.

commentaries.author: Apringius of Beja
commentaries.content: Concious of his fraility and humanity this saint wept because he thought that there was no one worthy enough to understand or truly perceive these things.

commentaries.author: Bede
commentaries.content: wept. He was grieved, as recognising the common misery of the human race.

commentaries.author: Nerses of Lambron
commentaries.content: All wept who were lost in ignorance, men and those from the orders of the most pure and angelic ranks.

commentaries.author: Nicholas of Lyra
commentaries.content: In this weeping John indicates the desire to know the future course of the Church.

commentaries.author: Victorinus of Pettau
commentaries.content: Neither among the angels of heaven, nor among men in earth, nor among the souls of the saints in rest, save Christ the Son of God alone, whom he says that he saw as a Lamb standing as it were slain, having seven horns. What had not been then announced, and what the law had contemplated for Him by its various oblations and sacrifices, it behooved Himself to fulfil. And because He Himself was the testator, who had overcome death, it was just that Himself should be appointed the Lord's heir, that He should possess the substance of the dying man, that is, the human members.
